ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists for Tom Yum cream pasta specifically; closest parent 'pasta dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as the ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for Tom Yum cream pasta fusion dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 'Pasta (food)' used as closest parent; no specific concept for Tom Yum cream pasta exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tom Yum cream pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a full plate of cooked pasta coated in creamy Tom Yum sauce, as typically served at a café or casual dining restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 20420) combined with estimated Tom Yum cream sauce contribution derived from USDA FoodData Central component data for coconut cream and Thai spice paste
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct approved-source entry exists for Tom Yum cream pasta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 20420, ~157 kcal/100g cooked) with a proportional contribution from coconut cream sauce and Tom Yum aromatics. The dish is approximately 55% pasta and 45% sauce by weight in the served portion. The vegan version uses plant-based cream (coconut cream base). Values represent a reasonable mid-range estimate for the composite dish per 100 g as served. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked, white, average) GI ~47–49; coconut cream contribution lowers overall GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum cream pasta. GI estimated at 48 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked white pasta (GI ~47–49, low category). The addition of coconut cream and acidic lime juice in the Tom Yum sauce is consistent with literature showing fat and acid reduce glycemic response; GI is therefore conservatively set at 48 (low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 75.25) / 100 ≈ 36.1, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 21.5 × 3.5 = 75.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from Tom Yum paste and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1085 mg per 350 g serving), this dish contributes a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of 48 and a glycemic load of approximately 36 per standard serving, this dish produces a moderate glycemic response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with non-starchy vegetables to further attenuate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut cream and sodium from Tom Yum paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut cream base contributes approximately 5.1 g saturated fat per 100 g, which may raise LDL cholesterol with frequent consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency, and opt for the vegan version which avoids animal-derived saturated fats while still containing coconut-derived saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta with Tom Yum Cream Sauce is a Southeast Asian-Western fusion pasta dish popular in Singapore's café and casual dining scene. It consists of cooked pasta — typically spaghetti or linguine — tossed in a rich, aromatic cream sauce built on Tom Yum paste, which is a Thai-origin spice blend featuring lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chilli, and lime juice. The sauce is finished with coconut cream (or a plant-based cream alternative), giving it a smooth, slightly spicy, sour, and fragrant character that balances the heat of chilli with the richness of cream. The dish is available in a vegan version at no extra charge at Flavours by Saute (Funan, Singapore), using plant-based cream and omitting animal proteins. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~168 kcal per 100 g), moderate carbohydrates from pasta, and a notable saturated fat contribution from coconut cream. Its low glycemic index (~48) makes it a relatively moderate-GI choice compared to other pasta dishes, though the glycemic load per full serving is substantial due to portion size. Sodium content is elevated due to Tom Yum paste, making it a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is halal-friendly, dairy-free, and vegan in its plant-based form. It is typically served as a main course at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: What is Tom Yum cream pasta?
Calories in Tom Yum cream pasta

confidence_reason: No direct approved-source nutrient entry exists for Tom Yum cream pasta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked pasta and coconut cream, combined proportionally. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked white pasta. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 20420) and coconut cream, combined in approximate proportions reflecting the dish composition (~55% pasta, ~45% sauce). No direct approved-source entry exists for this specific fusion dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for plant-based coconut cream sauce with no hydrogenated fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero for the vegan plant-based version; naturally zero as no animal products are present in the vegan formulation). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; no fortified ingredients or animal sources present in the vegan formulation). 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are approximate. 4. The dish is described as having a plant-based vegan option available at no extra charge at Flavours by Saute, Funan, Singapore (field survey data listing). The vegan version uses plant-based cream instead of dairy cream. 5. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white pasta GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; the acidic and fat-rich Tom Yum cream sauce is expected to maintain or slightly lower the GI relative to plain pasta. 6. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant plate; actual serving size may vary by restaurant. 7. Sodium estimate of 310 mg/100g reflects typical Tom Yum paste sodium contribution; actual sodium may vary significantly depending on the amount of paste and seasoning used. 8. The food_name has been canonicalised from the menu name 'Pasta Tom Yummy Cream' to 'Pasta with Tom Yum Cream Sauce' for clarity and searchability. The original menu name is preserved in aliases. 9. This dish is classified as Global/Fusion cuisine originating in Singapore, combining Italian pasta with Thai Tom Yum flavour profile. Multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Thai given the Thai culinary influence. 10. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA component data; no -1 values remain in this record.
